230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.
300	
301	## Logging
302	
303	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-writer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
304	
305	```markdown
306	# Blueprint Writer Report
307	
308	## Slug
309	<slug>
310	
311	## Status
312	<COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE>
313	<If INCOMPLETE: which required items could not be written and why.>
314	
315	## Target chapter
316	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
317	
318	## Changes Made
319	- **Added definition** `\definition`/`\label{def:foo}`/`\lean{Foo.bar}` — <one line on what it captures>
320	- **Added theorem** `\theorem`/`\label{thm:foo}` — <one line on statement>
321	  - Proof sketch added: <Y/N + brief shape>
322	- **Revised** `<existing label>` — <one line on what changed>
323	- **Fixed dependencies** `<label>` — corrected/added `\uses{...}` (was isolated / had a broken ref per leandag).
324	- **Removed** `<label>` — orphaned isolated block, removal authorized by directive.
325	- ...
326	
327	## Cross-references introduced
328	- `\uses{thm:bar}` added in proof of `\thm:foo` — verify `thm:bar` exists in <chapter or this same one>
329	- ...
330	
331	## References consulted
332	<every local file under `references/` you OPENED and READ this session, with a one-line note on what you took from each. The plan agent and the reviewer use this list to verify that every `% SOURCE:` parenthetical points at a file you actually read. Omit this section only when you wrote zero citation blocks (Archon-original chapter).>
333	- `references/hartshorne-III-5.md` — verbatim quote for `\thm:smooth_criterion` (statement + proof).
334	- `references/stacks-tag-01V4.md` — verbatim quote for `\def:etale_morphism`.
335	
336	## Macros needed (if any)
337	- `\foo{...}` — used in <where>; needs definition in `macros/common.tex`. NOT added by me (out of write-domain).
338	
339	## Reference-retriever dispatches (if any)
340	- slug `<child-slug>`: requested `<source>`. Status: COMPLETE / NOT_FOUND / PARTIAL. Summary at `references/<slug>.md`.
341	- ...
342	
343	## Notes for Plan Agent
344	- <any inconsistency I noticed in sibling chapters that the directive didn't cover>
345	- <any structural concern: e.g. "the chapter is now 800 lines, consider splitting">
346	- <any reference I had to guess at because the directive's reference section was incomplete>
347	
348	## Strategy-modifying findings
349	<populate this section ONLY if writing the chapter surfaced a need to
350	change the project's strategy itself — not just the chapter content.
351	Examples:
352	- A definition you were asked to write turns out to require a property
353	  that the strategy assumes is automatic but is not.
354	- A theorem you sketched is provable as stated, but its consequence
355	  used elsewhere in STRATEGY.md does not follow from it.
356	- A reference cited as authoritative actually requires a different
357	  setup than the strategy assumes.
358	
359	When this section is non-empty, the plan agent must update STRATEGY.md

361	chapter to paper over a strategy-level issue — surface it here.>

363	
364	## Return Value
365	
366	Your final assistant message:
367

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

773	\centerline{\large\bf Construction of Grassmannian}
774	
775	
776	\medskip
777	
778	The following explicit construction of 
779	the Grassmannian scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$ is best understood 
780	as the construction of a quotient
781	$GL_{d,\Z}\backslash V$, where $V$ is the scheme of all
782	$d\times r$ matrices of rank $d$, and the group-scheme
783	$GL_{d,\Z}$ acts on $V$ on the left by matrix multiplication. However,
784	we will not use the language of group-scheme actions 
785	here, instead, we give a direct elementary construction of 
786	the Grassmannian scheme.
787	
788	The reader can take $d=1$ in what follows, in a first reading, to
789	get the special case $\grass(r,1) = \PP^{r-1}_{\Z}$, which 
790	has another construction as $\proj \Z[x_1,\ldots,x_r]$. 
791	
792	
793	
794	\medskip
795	
796	
797	
798	\textbf{Construction by gluing together affine patches }
799	For any integers $r\ge d\ge 1$, the Grassmannian scheme
800	$\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$, together with the tautological 
801	quotient $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ where \
802	$\U$ is a rank $d$ locally free sheaf on $\grass(r,d)$, 
803	can be explicitly constructed as follows.
804	
805	
806	
807	If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and 
808	$I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal 
809	to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will 
810	mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
811	$I$. 
812	
813	
814	For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$, 
815	consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
816	$X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
817	entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$. 
818	Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
819	$x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically 
820	isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$. 
821	
822	For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let 
823	$P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
824	where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$.
825	Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
826	the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
827	the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
828	on $U^I_J$. 
829	
830	For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism 
831	$\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
832	defined as follows. The images of the variables 
833	$x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
834	$\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$.
835	In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
836	so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$.
837	
838	Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and 
839	we leave it to the reader to verify that 
840	for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of 
841	cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition 
842	$\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
843	is satisfied. Therefore the schemes 
844	$U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the 
845	${r\choose d}$ different 
846	subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
847	can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
848	finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
849	isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
850	$\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$.
851	
852	
853	
854	\medskip
855	
856	\textbf{Separatedness }
857	The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
858	can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ 
859	defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, 
860	and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme.
861	 
862	
863	\medskip
864

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).

111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}

140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
160	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
161	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
162	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
163	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
164	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
165	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
166	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
167	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
168	  %  the function
169	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
170	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
171	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
172	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
173	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
174	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
175	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
176	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
177	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
178	  \[
179	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
180	  \]
181	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
182	  the tensor product of sections
183	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
184	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
185	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
186	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
187	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
188	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
189	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
190	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
191	  polynomial ring).
192	\end{definition}
193	
194	\begin{definition}
195	[Section graded module]
196	  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
197	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
198	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing}
199	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
200	  % Polynomials" (read from
201	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
202	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
203	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
204	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
205	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
206	  %  the function
207	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
208	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
209	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
210	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
211	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
212	  I.7.}
213	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
214	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
215	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
216	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
217	  \[
218	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
219	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
220	  \]
221	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
222	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
223	  by the section tensor product
224	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
225	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
226	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
227	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
228	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
229	\end{definition}

231	\begin{lemma}
232	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
233	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
234	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
235	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
236	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
237	  % Polynomials" (read from
238	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
239	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
240	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
241	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
242	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
243	  %  the function
244	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
245	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
246	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
247	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
248	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
249	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
250	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
251	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
252	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
253	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
254	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
255	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
256	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
257	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
258	\end{lemma}
259	
260	\begin{proof}
261	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
262	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
263	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
264	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
265	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
266	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
267	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
268	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
269	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
270	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
271	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
272	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
273	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
274	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
275	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
276	\end{proof}
277	
278	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
279	  graded series]
280	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
281	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
282	  \mathlibok
283	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
284	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
285	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
286	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
287	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
288	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
289	  equals \(h(n)\):
290	  \[
291	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
292	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
293	  \]
294	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
295	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
296	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
297	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
298	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
299	\end{lemma}
300	
301	\begin{lemma}[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
302	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
303	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
304	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule, lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
305	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
306	  % Polynomials" (read from
307	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L459-L464).
308	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
309	  % "$${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
310	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
311	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
312	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that $\chi(F(m))$
313	  %  is indeed a polynomial in $m$ under the above assumption is a special
314	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof)."
315	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Hilbert--Serre,
316	  [Hartshorne], I.7.}
317	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
318	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
319	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
320	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
321	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
322	  such that for all \(n > N\)
323	  \[
324	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
325	  \]
326	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
327	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
328	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\). Applied to \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and
329	  \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (finitely generated by
330	  \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), this exhibits the graded Hilbert function
331	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
332	  as the eventual coefficient function of such a rational series.
333	
334	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
335	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
336	  module). It is a project-side obligation: Mathlib's
337	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} supplies only the converse
338	  polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not the production of the
339	  rational series from the module.
340	\end{lemma}
341	
342	\begin{proof}
343	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
344	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the ring \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) is Noetherian
345	  and finitely generated over \(\kappa = \kappa(s)\), say by homogeneous
346	  generators \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) of positive degrees, and
347	  \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
348	  with finite-dimensional components. Passing to a Veronese subalgebra and
349	  re-grading reduces to the case where \(R\) is generated in degree \(1\), so
350	  \(R\) is a graded quotient of a polynomial ring
351	  \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\). One proves rationality of the Hilbert series by
352	  induction on \(r\): multiplication by \(x_r\) gives a short exact sequence of
353	  graded modules
354	  \(0 \to K \to M(-1) \xrightarrow{x_r} M \to C \to 0\)
355	  with \(K, C\) finitely generated graded modules annihilated by \(x_r\), hence
356	  modules over a polynomial ring in \(r - 1\) variables; additivity of
357	  \(\dim_\kappa\) on the graded components turns the inductive hypothesis for
358	  \(K, C\) into the statement that
359	  \((1 - X) \cdot \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n\) is, up to a polynomial, of the
360	  form \(q \cdot (1 - X)^{-(d-1)}\), whence
361	  \(\sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n = p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) up to a polynomial. The
362	  base case \(r = 0\) has \(M\) finite-dimensional, so \(\dim_\kappa M_n = 0\) for
363	  \(n \gg 0\) and the series is a polynomial. Choosing \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\)
364	  (the integer coefficients viewed in \(\mathbb{Q}\)) gives the claim.
365	\end{proof}
366	
367	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
368	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
369	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
370	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
371	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
372	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
373	  % Polynomials" (read from
374	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
375	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
376	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
377	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
378	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
379	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
380	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
381	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
382	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
383	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
384	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
385	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
386	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
387	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
388	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
389	  \[
390	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
391	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
392	  \]
393	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
394	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
395	\end{theorem}
396	
397	\begin{proof}
398	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
399	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
400	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
401	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
402	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components. By
403	  \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
404	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
405	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
406	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
407	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
408	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
409	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
410	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
411	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
412	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
413	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
414	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
415	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
416	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
417	\end{proof}

683	\begin{proof}
684	  
685	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
686	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
687	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
688	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
689	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
690	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
691	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
692	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
693	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
694	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
695	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
696	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
697	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
698	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
699	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
700	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
701	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
702	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
703	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
704	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
705	
706	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
707	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
708	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
709	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
710	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
711	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
712	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
713	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
714	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
715	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
716	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
717	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
718	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
719	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
720	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism \(T \to U^I \subset
721	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Base change from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to a general noetherian
722	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
723	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
724	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
725	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
726	\end{proof}

% \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) is built directly by gluing
% \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)
% along explicit matrix-algebra transition maps, and shown smooth, separated
% and proper over \(\mathbb{Z}\). The later sub-phases (GR-quot: tautological
% quotient; GR-repr: functor-of-points; and the relative-over-\(S\) version
% with a vector bundle \(V\)) are OUT OF SCOPE here and are blueprinted with
% def:grassmannian_scheme / thm:grassmannian_representable in
% chapter Picard_QuotScheme. The declarations below are deliberately disjoint
% from that chapter's: this chapter adds only the construction lemmas for the
% absolute base \(\mathbb{Z}\).

\section{Set-up: charts indexed by \texorpdfstring{$d$}{d}-subsets}
\label{sec:gr_setup}

Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).

\begin{definition}
[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
  %  $I$.
  %
  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
  set
  \[
    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
  \]
  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
\end{definition}

\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
\label{sec:gr_transition}

\begin{definition}
[Transition map \(\theta_{I,J}\)]
  \label{def:gr_transition}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap}
  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
  % affine patches" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let
  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$.
  %  Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
  %  the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
  %  the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
  %  on $U^I_J$.
  %
  %  For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism
  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
  %  defined as follows. The images of the variables
  %  $x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
  %  $\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$.
  %  In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
  For \(I, J\) of cardinality \(d\), set
  \(P^I_J := \det(X^I_J) \in \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the determinant of the \(J\)-th
  minor of \(X^I\), and let
  \[
    U^I_J \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]
  \]
  be the principal open subscheme of \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) on
  which \(P^I_J\) is invertible; over \(U^I_J\) the matrix \(X^I_J\) is
  invertible, with inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) whose entries lie in
  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) by Cramer's rule. Note \(P^I_I = 1\), so
  \(U^I_I = U^I\). The \emph{transition map} \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the ring
  homomorphism
  \[
    \theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \longrightarrow
    \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
    \qquad
    \theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I,
  \]
  i.e.\ the indeterminate \(x^J_{p,q}\) maps to the \((p,q)\)-entry of the
  \(d \times r\) matrix \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\). This is well defined: the
  \(J\)-minor of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J = 1_{d\times d}\),
  matching the identity \(J\)-block of \(X^J\), and the \(I\)-minor of
  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\), so
  \[
    \theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J
  \]
  is invertible in the target; hence \(\theta_{I,J}\) extends from
  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]\) to the localisation \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I]\). Dually,
  \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the comorphism of a morphism of schemes
  \(U^I_J \to U^J_I\), which we also denote \(\theta_{I,J}\); it sends the
  matrix \(X^I\) to \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\). One has \(\theta_{I,I} = \id_{U^I}\).
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}
[Cocycle condition for the transition maps]
  \label{lem:gr_cocycle}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.cocycleCondition}
  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
  % affine patches" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
  %  we leave it to the reader to verify that
  %  for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
  %  cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
  %  $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
  %  is satisfied."
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
  For any three subsets \(I, J, K \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) of cardinality
  \(d\), the transition maps of \cref{def:gr_transition} satisfy the cocycle
  condition. As morphisms of schemes, on the triple overlap inside \(U^I\),
  \[
    \theta_{I,K} \;=\; \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J},
  \]
  equivalently, as ring homomorphisms,
  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) (the two read the same
  identity through the order-reversal of \(\Spec\)). Together with
  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\), this is exactly the compatibility datum required to
  glue the charts.
\end{lemma}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
% we leave it to the reader to verify that
% for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
% cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
% $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
% is satisfied."  (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
% L838-L848.)
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart}
  We verify the scheme-morphism form
  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) by a direct matrix
  computation, valid over the open locus of \(U^I\) on which all the minors
  involved are invertible (namely \(P^I_J\) and \(P^I_K\), which suffices since
  the maps are determined by their restriction to a dense open and all rings are
  domains; the computation is an identity of matrices with entries in
  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J, 1/P^I_K]\)).

  Start from the matrix \(X^I\) (the universal point of \(U^I\)). By
  \cref{def:gr_transition},
  \[
    M^J \;:=\; \theta_{I,J}(X^I) \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
  \]
  a \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(J\)-minor is the identity, hence a point of
  \(U^J\). Its \(K\)-minor is
  \[
    M^J_K \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_K,
    \qquad\text{so}\qquad
    (M^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J,
  \]
  using \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\) for the invertible \(d \times d\) matrices
  \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\). Applying \(\theta_{J,K}\) to \(M^J\) gives
  \[
    \theta_{J,K}(M^J) \;=\; (M^J_K)^{-1} M^J
    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J \,\cdot\, (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I
    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I,
  \]
  the middle product \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\) cancelling by
  associativity of matrix multiplication. The right-hand side is precisely
  \(\theta_{I,K}(X^I) = (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\). Thus
  \(\theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,K}\) agree on the
  universal matrix \(X^I\), hence agree as morphisms \(U^I \supseteq U^I_J \cap
  U^I_K \to U^K\). The special case \(K = I\) (resp.\ \(J = I\)) together with
  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\) shows each \(\theta_{I,J}\) is an isomorphism onto its
  image with inverse \(\theta_{J,I}\), since
  \(\theta_{J,I} \circ \theta_{I,J} = \theta_{I,I} = \id\).
\end{proof}

\section{The glued Grassmannian scheme}
\label{sec:gr_glued}

\begin{definition}
[The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
  \label{def:gr_glued_scheme}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scheme}
  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
  % affine patches" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L843-L850).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Therefore the schemes
  %  $U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the
  %  ${r\choose d}$ different
  %  subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
  %  finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
  %  isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$."
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
  Gluing the affine charts \(\{U^I\}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), one for each
  of the \(\binom{r}{d}\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) of cardinality
  \(d\), along the open subschemes \(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\) via the transition
  isomorphisms \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\)
  (\cref{def:gr_transition}) -- which satisfy the cocycle condition
  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\)
  of \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} -- yields a scheme
  \[
    \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \longrightarrow \Spec \mathbb{Z}.
  \]
  It is of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\) (a finite gluing of finitely
  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras), and under the gluing each \(U^I\) is an
  open subscheme of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J\).
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}
[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is smooth over \(\mathbb{Z}\) of relative dimension
  \(d(r-d)\)]
  \label{lem:gr_smooth}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.smooth}
  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
  % affine patches" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L848-L850).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "As each $U^I$ is
  %  isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$."
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
  The structure morphism \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is smooth of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\).
\end{lemma}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "As each $U^I$ is
% isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
% $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$."
% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L848-L850.)
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_glued_scheme}
  Smoothness and relative dimension are local on the source. The charts
  \(\{U^I\}\) form an open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}), and each \(U^I \cong
  \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is the affine
  space of dimension \(d(r-d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\), which is smooth over
  \(\mathbb{Z}\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\) (the polynomial ring
  \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_{d(r-d)}]\) is a smooth \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra).
  Since smoothness of a fixed relative dimension is preserved under restriction
  to an open cover of the source and the property may be checked on that cover,
  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
  \(d(r-d)\).
\end{proof}

\section{Separatedness}
\label{sec:gr_separated}

\begin{lemma}
[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
  \label{lem:gr_separated}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isSeparated}
  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Separatedness" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
  %  can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$
  %  defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$,
  %  and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
  The structure morphism \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is separated; equivalently, the diagonal
  \(\Delta : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a closed immersion.
\end{lemma}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with
% $U^I\times U^J$ can be seen to be the closed subscheme
% $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
% $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860.)
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
  The products \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), as \(I, J\) range over the
  size-\(d\) subsets, form an affine open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
  \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Separatedness may be checked on this
  cover: it suffices to show that for each pair \((I,J)\) the preimage
  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J)\) maps by a closed immersion into
  \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), i.e.\ that the corresponding ring
  homomorphism is surjective.

  By \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme} the diagonal preimage is the overlap
  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J) = U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J = \Spec
  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\). The restricted diagonal corresponds to the ring map
  \[
    \delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]
    \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
    \qquad
    X^I \otimes 1 \mapsto X^I, \quad 1 \otimes X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
  \]
  the second component being the comorphism \(\theta_{I,J}\) of
  \cref{def:gr_transition} (a point of \(U^I_J\) and its image under
  \(\theta_{I,J}\) are the two coordinates of a point of the diagonal). We claim
  \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Its image contains all entries of \(X^I\)
  (from the first factor). From the second factor it contains the entries of
  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\); taking the \(I\)-minor of this matrix, and using
  \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\), the image contains the entries of \((X^I_J)^{-1}\),
  and in particular
  \[
    \delta_{I,J}(1 \otimes P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J .
  \]
  Hence \(1/P^I_J\) lies in the image, so the image is all of
  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) and \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Concretely the
  closed subscheme \(\Delta_{I,J} \subseteq U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\) is cut
  out by the entries of the matrix relation
  \[
    X^J_I \, X^I - X^J \;=\; 0,
  \]
  which on the diagonal holds because \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) forces
  \(X^J_I = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) and therefore \(X^J_I X^I =
  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I = X^J\). As each restricted diagonal is a closed immersion,
  \(\Delta\) is a closed immersion and \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over
  \(\mathbb{Z}\).
\end{proof}

\section{Properness}
\label{sec:gr_proper}

\begin{lemma}
[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is proper over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
  \label{lem:gr_proper}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isProper}
  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Properness" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L865-L891).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper. It is enough to
  %  verify the valuative criterion of properness for discrete valuation rings.
  %  Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let
  %  $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$ be a morphism. This is given by
  %  a ring homomorphism $f : \Z[X^I] \to \K$ for some $I$. Having fixed
  %  one such $I$, next choose $J$ such that $\nu(f(P^I_J))$ is minimum,
  %  where $\nu : \K \to \Z\bigcup \{ \infty\}$ denotes
  %  the discrete valuation. As $P^I_I = 1$, note that
  %  $\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore
  %  $f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$ and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$
  %  lies in $GL_d(\K)$.
  %
  %  Now consider the homomorphism
  %  $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
  %  $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$
  %  Then $g$ defines the same morphism $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$,
  %  and moreover all $d\times d$ minors $X^J_K$ satisfy
  %  $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$.
  %  As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it follows from the above that in fact
  %  $\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of $X^J$.
  %  Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$
  %  factors uniquely via $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of
  %  schemes $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
  %  $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked
  %  separatedness of $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that
  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper."
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
  The structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is proper.
\end{lemma}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is
% proper. It is enough to verify the valuative criterion of properness for
% discrete valuation rings. Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let
% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$ be a morphism. This is given by a ring
% homomorphism $f : \Z[X^I] \to \K$ for some $I$. Having fixed one such $I$, next
% choose $J$ such that $\nu(f(P^I_J))$ is minimum, where
% $\nu : \K \to \Z\bigcup \{ \infty\}$ denotes the discrete valuation. As
% $P^I_I = 1$, note that $\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore $f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$
% and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$ lies in $GL_d(\K)$. Now consider the homomorphism
% $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
% $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$ Then $g$ defines the same morphism
% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$, and moreover all $d\times d$ minors
% $X^J_K$ satisfy $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$. As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it
% follows from the above that in fact $\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of
% $X^J$. Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ factors uniquely via
% $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of schemes
% $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked separatedness of
% $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper."
% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L865-L891.)
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated, def:gr_transition}
  Since \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is of finite type
  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) and separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), and
  \(\mathbb{Z}\) is Noetherian, properness follows from the valuative criterion
  for discrete valuation rings: it suffices to show that every solid diagram
  \[
    \begin{array}{ccc}
      \Spec K & \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } & \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \\
      \downarrow & & \downarrow \pi \\
      \Spec R & \longrightarrow & \Spec \mathbb{Z}
    \end{array}
  \]
  with \(R\) a discrete valuation ring, \(K = \operatorname{Frac}(R)\) and
  valuation \(\nu : K \to \mathbb{Z} \cup \{\infty\}\), admits a unique diagonal
  filler \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).

  \emph{Existence.} The morphism \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) lands
  in some chart \(U^I\) (\(\Spec K\) has one point), so it is given by a ring map
  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\). Among the \(\binom{r}{d}\) minors choose \(J\)
  with \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) minimal. Because \(P^I_I = 1\) gives \(f(P^I_I) = 1\)
  with \(\nu = 0\), the minimum satisfies \(\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0\); in particular
  \(f(P^I_J) \ne 0\), so \(f(X^I_J) \in \mathrm{GL}_d(K)\) is invertible over
  \(K\). Define
  \[
    g : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \longrightarrow K, \qquad
    g(X^J) = f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr) ;
  \]
  this is the composite of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) with
  \(f\), so \(g\) defines the same \(K\)-point \(\varphi\), now exhibited as
  landing in \(U^J\).

  We show every entry \(g(x^J_{p,q})\) lies in \(R\), i.e.\ has \(\nu \ge 0\).
  First, for any size-\(d\) subset \(K' \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) the \(K'\)-minor
  of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\), so
  \[
    g(P^J_{K'}) = \det f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\bigr)
    = \frac{f(P^I_{K'})}{f(P^I_J)},
    \qquad
    \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) = \nu(f(P^I_{K'})) - \nu(f(P^I_J)) \ge 0
  \]
  by minimality of \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\). Now the \(J\)-minor \(X^J_J\) of \(X^J\) is
  the identity, so by cofactor expansion each individual entry
  \(x^J_{p,q}\) (with \(q \notin J\)) equals, up to sign, the minor
  \(P^J_{K'}\) for \(K' = (J \setminus \{j_p\}) \cup \{q\}\) -- the \(d \times d\)
  determinant obtained by replacing the \(p\)-th identity column of \(X^J_J\) by
  the column \(q\). Hence \(\nu(g(x^J_{p,q})) = \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) \ge 0\) for all
  entries, and the entries with \(q \in J\) are \(0\) or \(1\). Therefore
  \(g(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]) \subseteq R\), so \(g\) factors uniquely as
  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R \hookrightarrow K\). The corresponding morphism
  \(\Spec R \to U^J \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) restricts to \(\varphi\)
  over \(\Spec K\) and lies over \(\Spec \mathbb{Z}\), providing the filler.

  \emph{Uniqueness.} Any two fillers agree over the generic point \(\Spec K\)
  and, \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) being separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)
  (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), two morphisms \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
  agreeing on the schematically dense open \(\Spec K\) coincide. This verifies
  the valuative criterion, so \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is
  proper.
\end{proof}

\section{Out of scope}
\label{sec:gr_out_of_scope}

This chapter constructs the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
the absolute base \(\mathbb{Z}\) and establishes its smoothness, separatedness
and properness -- the GR-cells and GR-glue sub-phases. The following are
deliberately deferred to later sub-phases and are not treated here:
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{The tautological/universal quotient} \(u : \mathcal{O}^{\oplus
    r}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) and the determinant
    line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\) with its Pl\"ucker embedding (GR-quot).
  \item \textbf{The functor-of-points representability} of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
    -- that it represents \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d) =
    \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^r \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}} /
    \mathbb{Z} / \mathbb{Z}}\) (GR-repr); this is blueprinted as
    \cref{thm:grassmannian_representable} in chapter Picard\_QuotScheme.
  \item \textbf{The relative version over a general base \(S\)} with a locally
    free sheaf \(V\) of rank \(r\): the scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V,d)\) obtained by
    base change and gluing over a trivialising cover of \(V\). The
    absolute-over-\(\mathbb{Z}\) construction of this chapter is the input to
    that base change.
\end{itemize}

  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
  \(d(r-d)\).
\end{proof}
